title: Mad or Bad? Race, Class, Gender, and Mental Disorder in the Criminal Justice System By Melissa Thompson LFB Scholarly Publishing LLC, 2010, 197 pages. $32.95 paperback
abstract: The deinstituionalization and medicalization of the mentally ill coincide with the growth of the criminal justice system. These trends suggest a potential increase in the number of people with mental health disorders moving in and out of the criminal justice system. Melissa Thompson's Mad or Bad? Race, Class, Gender, and Mental Disorder in the Criminal Justice System is an ambitious effort to provide a sociological analysis of the attribution of mental illness in the criminal justice system. Thompson argues that there are good reasons to believe that mechanisms of social control may vary by race and gender with women more likely to have their behavior constructed through the lens of mental illness, while African Americans may be viewed as more violent and less likely to suffer from mental health problems. If female offenders are more likely to be viewed as “mad” and African American offenders as normally criminal or “bad,” what does this mean for African American females?
